scene_description: Jordan Belfort's opening sales pitch to his first Long Island boiler room. He locks eyes with each person in the room, holds direct gaze while speaking, never looks away mid-sentence. Textbook confident eye contact — obvious and high-energy.
search_queries: Wolf of Wall Street boiler room sales pitch scene
Wolf of Wall Street Jordan Belfort eye contact confidence
Wolf of Wall Street first job stockbroker scene
context_note: Look at how Belfort holds eye contact with each listener for 2–3 full seconds before moving on. This is the basic pattern.

scene_description: Chris Gardner walks into the Dean Witter job interview in paint-stained clothes. Despite his situation, he maintains steady professional eye contact with the panel throughout — holding gaze under clear social pressure.
search_queries: Pursuit of Happyness job interview scene
Will Smith job interview Pursuit of Happyness
Pursuit of Happyness Dean Witter interview paint clothes
context_note: Notice how eye contact is used as a social equalizer — it projects confidence even when status is unequal.

scene_description: The bowling alley scene. Plainview's final confrontation with Eli Sunday. His eye contact is weaponized — he holds still, barely blinks, forces Eli to break gaze first. Dominance through stillness. Advanced because the aggression is almost entirely in the eyes.
search_queries: There Will Be Blood bowling alley scene Eli Sunday
There Will Be Blood I'm finished Daniel Plainview
There Will Be Blood final scene Daniel Day-Lewis
context_note: Study how stillness amplifies eye contact power. He doesn't reach out — he pulls with his gaze.

scene_description: The No Man's Land scene. Diana climbs out of the trench and walks slowly toward enemy lines in full armor. Shoulders back, arms slightly out, spine straight, face forward. The most cinematically obvious open-posture demonstration in modern cinema.
search_queries: Wonder Woman no man's land scene
Wonder Woman trench scene posture
Gal Gadot Wonder Woman walks toward bullets
context_note: Notice the arms slightly away from the body, the full upright spine. Count how many cues are stacked: posture + gaze + pace.

scene_description: Lionel Logue coaching Bertie in the studio. Bertie starts collapsed and closed. Lionel physically repositions him — arms out, chin up, weight centered. We watch the transformation from closed to open posture in real time.
search_queries: King's Speech Lionel coaching posture scene
King's Speech speech therapy posture Bertie
Colin Firth Geoffrey Rush King's Speech coaching
context_note: Watch the before/after. Closed posture compresses the voice. Open posture unlocks it.

scene_description: Lincoln in the small back room persuading two holdout congressmen. He sits in a relaxed, wide open posture — not performing confidence but fully inhabiting it. The openness reads as unshakeable, not effortful.
search_queries: Lincoln 2012 Daniel Day-Lewis congress persuasion scene
Lincoln film holdout vote scene
Steven Spielberg Lincoln amendment lobbying scene
context_note: Advanced because the posture is understated. He's not posing — it's fully internalized. Study the seat width, elbow placement, and absence of tension.

scene_description: Sean's 'It's not your fault' therapy scene. Robin Williams holds still, leans slightly forward, full face engagement, zero interruption. Will tries to deflect with humor multiple times — Sean just waits. Perfect active listening demonstration.
search_queries: Good Will Hunting it's not your fault scene
Robin Williams Good Will Hunting therapy park bench
Good Will Hunting emotional scene Sean Will
context_note: Count how many times Sean nods, maintains eye contact, and stays silent. These are the three core mechanics of active listening.

scene_description: Eduardo in the deposition room, listening to the lawyer read the dilution agreement aloud. He listens with controlled, focused attention — not reacting outwardly despite the emotional content. Active listening under pressure.
search_queries: Social Network deposition scene Eduardo Saverin
Social Network dilution agreement deposition
Andrew Garfield Social Network listening scene
context_note: Intermediate because the emotional stakes suppress natural expressions. Notice how he leans forward only slightly — physical attention without over-signaling.

scene_description: Andrew's first private session with Fletcher. Fletcher plays a phrase, Andrew listens, tries to match it on drums. The way Andrew watches — posture, head angle, micro-nods — is elite active listening. Fletcher exploits every missed signal.
search_queries: Whiplash first rehearsal scene Fletcher Andrew drums
Whiplash private lesson scene
Miles Teller J.K. Simmons Whiplash first session
context_note: Advanced: the 'listening' is partially concealed by physical activity. Watch for micro-signals only — the tilt, the stillness between notes.

scene_description: O Captain, My Captain scene. Keating speaks in short, deliberate sentences with clear pauses between ideas. Not rushing to fill silence. Each pause lands before the next idea arrives. Textbook pacing.
search_queries: Dead Poets Society O Captain My Captain scene
Robin Williams Dead Poets Society standing on desk
Dead Poets Society final scene students stand
context_note: Listen to the gaps between 'O Captain' repetitions. Each pause is intentional — it gives weight to what follows.

scene_description: Bertie's final wartime radio broadcast. He begins halting and over-paced, then finds his rhythm. The strategic use of pauses — covering stutter but landing as gravitas — is masterful. Demonstrates how pacing controls audience emotion.
search_queries: King's Speech final wartime broadcast speech
King's Speech radio speech scene climax
Colin Firth King's Speech 'I am speaking to you from the Cabinet Room'
context_note: Notice how the longest pauses come before the most important words. Pause = emphasis in vocal pacing.

scene_description: The 'I drink your milkshake' / 'Drainage!' monologue. Plainview alternates between slow, deliberate explanation and sudden explosive speed — using pacing as a weapon. The silence before 'I'm finished' is one of cinema's greatest pauses.
search_queries: There Will Be Blood I drink your milkshake scene
There Will Be Blood drainage monologue Daniel Day-Lewis
There Will Be Blood milkshake bowling alley full scene
context_note: Advanced: pacing is weaponized — he speeds up to unsettle, slows to make Eli feel each word. Count the seconds of silence before 'I'm finished'.

scene_description: Juror 8 (Henry Fonda) casts the lone 'not guilty' vote in the first ballot. Eleven men stare at him. He doesn't shrink. Open posture, calm voice, no aggression — just a clear, grounded position held without apology.
search_queries: 12 Angry Men not guilty vote scene Henry Fonda
12 Angry Men first vote lone dissenter
12 Angry Men 1957 Henry Fonda jury room
context_note: Notice: no justification, no apology. He says 'not guilty' and simply holds the silence that follows. That stillness is the skill.

scene_description: Kaffee's cross-examination of Colonel Jessep. He pushes back against an intimidating, superior-ranking officer with evidence-backed disagreement. Open body toward the witness, voice never rises, holds position when Jessep gets aggressive.
search_queries: A Few Good Men you can't handle the truth cross examination
Tom Cruise cross examines Jack Nicholson A Few Good Men
A Few Good Men courtroom scene Kaffee Jessep
context_note: Watch Kaffee's body. As Jessep escalates, Kaffee's posture opens slightly more. He doesn't back up physically or vocally.

scene_description: Eduardo confronts Mark in the Facebook office after being diluted to 0.03%. He walks in, places the laptop on the desk, states his position clearly, and refuses to be managed or minimized. The boardroom exit is the advanced technique: leaving while maintaining dignity.
search_queries: Social Network Eduardo confronts Mark Facebook office
Social Network Eduardo diluted shares confrontation
Andrew Garfield Jesse Eisenberg Social Network final meeting
context_note: Advanced: disagreement while managing grief, anger, and humiliation simultaneously. Watch how the body stays open even as his voice breaks.
